metadata.subject: Career Education
metadata.description: Career-Life Connections is a required 4-credit course that forms the capstone of BC's Career Education program. Students synthesize all of their learning, experience, and self-knowledge into a Capstone Project that demonstrates their readiness for post-graduation life — whether that means post-secondary education, trades, work, or community engagement. Students engage in structured reflection on Core Competencies, create a final career-life plan, and present their learning journey. First Peoples perspectives on purpose, community contribution, relational knowledge, and the interconnectedness of life and work are central. This course is required for graduation.
metadata.general_description: The required capstone career education course for BC graduation — synthesizing career-life learning, Core Competencies, experiential learning, and future planning into a Capstone Project and learning journey presentation.

big_ideas.statement: Career-life development includes ongoing cycles of exploring, planning, reflecting, adapting, and deciding.
big_ideas.elaboration: The capstone experience makes visible the full arc of students' career-life development — a spiral process of learning, trying, reflecting, and adjusting that they will continue long after graduation.

big_ideas.statement: Career-life decisions influence and are influenced by internal and external factors.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students at graduation are well positioned to analyze the complex interplay between personal factors (values, strengths, interests, identity) and external realities (labour market, family, community, and global trends) in shaping realistic and meaningful post-graduation plans.

big_ideas.statement: Engaging in networks and reciprocal relationships with family, social groups, local community, post-secondary education communities, professional communities, digital communities, and the global community can guide and broaden career-life awareness and options.
big_ideas.elaboration: At graduation, students can draw on the full breadth of relationships they have cultivated — and can articulate how these networks have shaped their development and will continue to support their futures.

big_ideas.statement: A sense of purpose and career-life balance support well-being.
big_ideas.elaboration: Meaningful post-graduation life requires both a sense of purpose — a direction that aligns with one's values and strengths — and sustainable practices for maintaining well-being across all life domains.

big_ideas.statement: Lifelong learning and active citizenship foster career-life opportunities for people and communities.
big_ideas.elaboration: Graduation is not the end of learning — it is a transition into broader communities of learning and civic life. Students who understand themselves as lifelong learners and engaged citizens are best positioned for a fulfilling future.

competencies.raw: Recognize personal worldviews and perspectives, considering their influence on values, actions, and preferred futures; analyze internal and external factors to inform personal career-life choices for post-graduation planning; assess personal transferable skills, and identify strengths and those requiring refinement; explore and evaluate personal strategies to maintain well-being.
i_can_statements: I can recognize my own worldview and how my values, cultural background, and experiences shape my vision of a preferred future.
I can analyze both internal factors (strengths, values, interests) and external factors (labour market, family, community) in making post-graduation plans.
I can honestly assess my transferable skills — identifying genuine strengths and areas I need to continue developing.
I can evaluate the well-being strategies I have developed and articulate how I will maintain balance in my post-graduation life.

competencies.raw: Collaborate with a mentor to inform career-life development and exploration; engage with personal, education, and employment networks to cultivate post-graduation resources and social capital; create and critique personal and public profiles for self-advocacy and marketing; demonstrate and reflect on inclusive, respectful, and safe interactions in multiple career-life contexts.
i_can_statements: I can collaborate with a mentor to clarify my post-graduation direction and develop my Capstone Project.
I can actively engage with personal, educational, and professional networks to cultivate resources and social capital for life after graduation.
I can create and critically evaluate professional profiles — resume, LinkedIn, portfolio, and digital presence — for self-advocacy and career marketing.
I can demonstrate and reflect on professional, inclusive, and safe conduct across diverse career-life contexts.

competencies.raw: Explore possibilities for preferred personal and education/employment futures; identify and apply preferred approaches to learning for ongoing development; engage in, reflect on, and evaluate career-life exploration.
i_can_statements: I can explore and articulate clear possibilities for my preferred post-graduation future — with specific goals and pathways.
I can identify my preferred approaches to learning and apply them in pursuing post-graduation growth.
I can engage in authentic career-life exploration and critically evaluate what I have learned about myself and the world of work.

competencies.raw: Reflect on experiences and assess Core Competencies development; design, assemble, and present a Capstone Project.
i_can_statements: I can reflect honestly and deeply on my experiences across all subject areas and activities and assess my Core Competencies development.
I can design, assemble, and present a Capstone Project that tells the story of my learning journey and demonstrates my readiness for life after graduation.
I can present my Capstone to an authentic audience — including community members and post-secondary representatives — with confidence and clarity.

areas.name: Personal Career-Life Development
areas.raw: Mentorship, Core Competencies, self-advocacy, identity, well-being, employment marketing, workplace rights.
topics: Capstone mentorship: working with a mentor to develop and refine the Capstone Project
Core Competencies: communication, thinking, personal and social responsibility — assessed and documented
Self-advocacy: communicating your story, needs, and value to post-secondary institutions and employers
Identity-shaping factors: exploring how culture, community, gender, disability, and other factors shape career-life
Well-being and work-life balance: sustainable practices for health, relationships, and personal renewal
Reflection strategies: metacognition, journaling, and evidence-based self-assessment
Employment marketing: polished resume, cover letter, portfolio, and LinkedIn or professional profile
Workplace rights and regulations: Employment Standards Act, Workers' Compensation, and human rights at work

areas.name: Connections with Community
areas.raw: Social capital, intercultural skills, career-life exploration, digital literacy, citizenship.
topics: Social capital: building and leveraging networks of relationships for post-graduation success
Transferable skills: communication, teamwork, critical thinking, adaptability — demonstrated through real experiences
Intercultural and leadership competencies: working across difference in diverse workplaces and communities
Career-life exploration: connecting final experiential learning to specific post-graduation directions
Self-representation and digital literacy: managing a professional online presence
Active citizenship: engagement in community, civic life, and environmental stewardship
First Peoples perspectives on community, reciprocity, and land-based career paths

areas.name: Career-Life Planning and Capstone
areas.raw: Self-assessment, portfolio, career-life roles, post-graduation planning, labour market, budget, Capstone.
topics: Comprehensive self-assessment: synthesizing all career-life learning into a coherent personal profile
Organizing authentic career-life evidence: curating a portfolio that tells a compelling story
Career-life roles and transitions: understanding the multiple roles people play across a lifetime
Post-graduation possibilities: finalized plans for college, university, trades, work, travel, or community service
Labour market trends and influences: BC and Canadian occupational demand, wages, and growth sectors
Budget planning: post-graduation financial projections, income goals, and lifestyle costs
Capstone Project guidelines: designing, developing, and presenting a project that meets BC Ministry requirements
Learning journey presentation: presenting to an authentic panel — the culminating graduation requirement
